groups.name: Pentastar V6 Family
groups.failureNote: The Pentastar 3.6L is Jeep's most widely used petrol V6. Known for cylinder head issues, rocker arm failures, and oil consumption. Get Quote → Get Quote → Common failure points: The Pentastar family is known for cylinder head cracking (early 2011-2013 units), rocker arm and camshaft follower failure causing misfire codes, and excessive oil consumption . Our rebuilt units include revised cylinder heads and upgraded rocker assemblies.

groups.name: MultiJet II Diesel Family
groups.failureNote: VM Motori's 2.0L and 2.2L turbo diesels power most modern Jeep diesel models. The 2.2L (EBU/EBV code) is prone to EGR and DPF issues. Get Quote → Get Quote → Get Quote → Common failure points: MultiJet II engines suffer from oil wicking through the engine wiring loom (contaminating the ECU), EGR valve carbon buildup, DPF clogging on short journeys, and timing chain stretch . Our rebuilt units include a new timing chain kit and EGR cleaning.

groups.name: CRD Diesel Family
groups.failureNote: VM Motori's 2.8L and 3.0L CRD engines. The 3.0L V6 is known for high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failure and injector issues. Get Quote → Get Quote → Common failure points: The 3.0 CRD V6 suffers from HPFP failure sending metal debris through the fuel system, injector clogging, and swirl flap failure . Standard rebuild includes HPFP replacement and full injector testing.

groups.name: HEMI V8 Family
groups.failureNote: The iconic HEMI V8 powers Grand Cherokee, Wrangler, and Wagoneer. The 5.7L is notorious for MDS lifter tick and camshaft wear. Get Quote → Get Quote → Get Quote → Common failure points: HEMI engines are known for MDS (Multiple Displacement System) lifter failure causing the infamous "HEMI tick" — collapsed lifters and camshaft lobe damage . Our rebuilt units use upgraded lifters and high-flow oil pumps.

groups.name: Tigershark & GME Petrol Family
groups.failureNote: The 2.4L Tigershark and 1.3L/2.0L GME turbo engines. The 2.4L is particularly notorious for excessive oil consumption. Get Quote → Get Quote → Get Quote → Common failure points: The 2.4L Tigershark suffers from excessive oil consumption (up to 1 quart per 1,000 miles) often caused by software calibration or piston ring issues . The 2.0L GME has had recalls over internal sand contamination from casting .

groups.name: Classic & Legacy Engines
groups.failureNote: For older Cherokee XJ, Wrangler YJ/TJ, and Comanche owners — these engines remain in high demand for rebuilds. Get Quote → Get Quote → Get Quote → Get Quote → Common failure points: The legendary 4.0L AMC I6 is generally reliable but suffers from rear main seal leaks and cracked cylinder heads on 2000-2001 models. The 2.5 TD (VM diesel) has head gasket and injection pump issues

commonProblems.h2: Common Jeep Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: Jeep ownership in the UK spans rugged off-road enthusiasts, fleet users, and family SUV drivers — but certain engine families have developed well-documented weaknesses. The 3.6L Pentastar’s oil consumption and timing chain stretch, the 2.2L MultiJet II’s EGR/DPF and timing chain failures, and the older 3.0L CRD’s oil cooler / turbo issues dominate replacement demand. Every rebuilt engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point — plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Below we break down the most common failures, what they cost to fix at a dealer versus a specialist, and when replacement actually makes financial sense.

problems.group: Pentastar 3.6L Oil Consumption & Timing Chain Stretch
problems.h4: Pentastar 3.6L Oil Consumption & Timing Chain Stretch – Why These Engines Fail Prematurely
problems.affectedModels: Jeep Wrangler (JK & JL, 2012–2023, 3.6L Pentastar), Grand Cherokee (WK2, 2011–2021, 3.6L Pentastar), Cherokee (KL, 2014–2022, 3.6L Pentastar), Promaster / Ram van-based models
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 60,000–110,000 miles (oil consumption often appears earlier, chain stretch by 80k)
problems.rootCause: The Pentastar’s cylinder deactivation (VVL) system combined with insufficient piston ring tension leads to excessive oil consumption, which then accelerates timing chain wear. The plastic chain guides become brittle, and the VVT phasers suffer oil starvation.

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – Preventative Oil Separator & PCV Replacement
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace PCV valve, oil separator, perform engine flush and oil change with correct spec | Reduces oil consumption if caught early (before chain stretch).
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable for sub‑60k miles with minor symptoms.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace timing chains (primary + secondary), guides, tensioners, VVT phasers, water pump | Restores cam timing reliability. Suitable when oil consumption is managed but chain has stretched. Labour-intensive.
| Supply & fit rebuilt engine with upgraded piston rings, revised timing components, bench tested, 12‑month warranty | Best long-term solution. Upgraded rings address original oil consumption.
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable for engines with internal damage or >100k miles.

problems.recommendation: For a Wrangler or Grand Cherokee with early oil consumption (under 70k miles), start with the PCV/oil separator replacement and monitor oil levels closely. If timing chain rattle appears on cold start, the intermediate repair is essential — but on any Pentastar over 100k miles with both oil consumption and chain noise, we recommend the full replacement with upgraded piston rings. The labour overlap makes the full swap better value than piecing repairs together.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt Pentastar 3.6L engine prices for your Wrangler or Grand Cherokee

problems.h4: MultiJet II 2.2L Timing Chain & EGR/DPF Failure – The Diesel Weak Point
problems.affectedModels: Jeep Cherokee (KL, 2014–2018, 2.2L MultiJet II), Renegade (BU, 2015–2019, 2.2L MultiJet II), Compass (2017–2022, 2.2L MultiJet II)
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 50,000–90,000 miles (timing chain often fails before 80k; EGR/DPF clogging from 50k)
problems.rootCause: The 2.2L MultiJet II uses a single-stage timing chain that stretches prematurely, especially on vehicles used for short trips. Simultaneously, the EGR system over-cokes the intake manifold and DPF, leading to backpressure that accelerates chain wear and turbo failure.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Chemical or media-blast decoke of EGR valve, intake manifold, and DPF forced regeneration | Restores airflow and reduces soot load.
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable for 40–70k miles with no timing chain symptoms.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace timing chain, guides, tensioners, crank sprocket, oil pump chain (recommended), and camshaft actuator | Essential if chain slack is detected. Prevents valve/piston contact. Combine with decoke for full benefit.

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt 2.2L MultiJet II Engine
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit rebuilt engine with uprated timing chain, revised EGR cooler, new injector seals, 12‑month warranty | Recommended if chain has jumped timing (fault codes P0016/P0017) or DPF is completely blocked (>90% soot load).

problems.recommendation: MultiJet II failures often start with the EGR system — a decoke at 50k miles can prevent the chain from overworking. However, once you hear chain rattle or get cam/crank correlation faults, do not drive the vehicle. The intermediate timing chain repair is viable up to 90k miles, but if the engine has ingested soot through the EGR or the DPF is beyond regeneration, replacement is the safer long-term route.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt 2.2L MultiJet II engines with uprated timing chains

problems.h4: 2.4L Tigershark MultiAir – Valve Train & Oil Burning Failure
problems.affectedModels: Jeep Renegade (BU, 2015–2021, 2.4L Tigershark), Cherokee (KL, 2014–2020, 2.4L Tigershark), Compass (2017–2022, 2.4L Tigershark)
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 50,000–90,000 miles (oil burning from 40k; MultiAir unit failure from 60k)
problems.rootCause: The MultiAir electro-hydraulic valve actuation system is sensitive to oil quality and viscosity. Sludge or low oil levels cause the MultiAir unit to fail, leading to cylinder misfires, valvetrain noise, and eventually burnt valves. The engine also has high natural oil consumption.

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – MultiAir Unit Replacement & Oil System Flush
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace MultiAir brick (actuator unit), perform oil system flush, replace oil filter and correct 5W‑20 oil | Restores valve control.
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable if caught early (misfire codes P0300–P0304 but no mechanical valve damage).

repairOptions.tier: | Major – Cylinder Head Rebuild With Valves
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Remove cylinder head, replace burnt exhaust valves, valve guides, MultiAir unit (again), head gasket set | Required if the engine has been driven with MultiAir failure for >500 miles. Labour-intensive but rebuilds the top end.

| Supply & fit rebuilt long block with updated MultiAir unit, upgraded valve seals, new oil pump, 12‑month warranty | Best option for engines with bottom-end wear (knocking, low oil pressure) or >100k miles where bore scoring is suspected.

problems.recommendation: The Tigershark is unforgiving of missed oil changes. If you have a Renegade or Compass with early MultiAir failure (rough idle, check engine light, no valve damage yet), the minor MultiAir replacement is absolutely the right call. But if the engine has been misfiring for hundreds of miles or you hear bottom-end knocking, skip the intermediate repair and go straight to a full replacement. The cost difference is smaller than you think, and the warranty on a rebuilt engine gives real peace of mind.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 2.4L Tigershark engine prices for Renegade & Cherokee

problems.h4: 3.0L CRD (VM Motori) – Oil Cooler, Turbo & Crankshaft Bearing Failure
problems.affectedModels: Jeep Grand Cherokee (WK2, 2011–2019, 3.0L CRD – OM642 / A642), Cherokee (pre‑2014, 3.0L CRD)
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 70,000–130,000 miles (oil cooler leaks from 70k; turbo failure from 90k; bearing failure 110k+)
problems.rootCause: The oil cooler housing (made of composite plastic) cracks, mixing oil and coolant and causing oil starvation to the turbo and crankshaft bearings. The turbo’s variable geometry vanes also coke up, and in later miles, the main bearings fail due to oil degradation.

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – Oil Cooler Housing & Turbo Inspection
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace oil cooler housing (uprated aluminium version recommended), flush cooling system, inspect turbo for coking | Prevents coolant contamination. Suitable for 70–100k miles with no turbo or bearing symptoms. Do NOT fit plastic again.
| Replace or rebuild turbo (VGT cleaned or new core), replace oil cooler (aluminium), oil pump drive, sump clean | Required if turbo has failed or oil is heavily contaminated.
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable where bearings are still healthy (no knocking).

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit rebuilt engine with uprated aluminium oil cooler housing, new turbo, crankshaft bearings, oil pump, 12‑month warranty | The only safe option if the engine has suffered bearing damage (deep knocking, low oil pressure) or the crank has been run with coolant-mixed oil.

problems.recommendation: The 3.0L CRD is a great engine when maintained, but the plastic oil cooler is a criminal design fault. On any Grand Cherokee over 70k miles, proactively replace the oil cooler with an aluminium aftermarket unit — it will save you thousands. If you already have turbo failure but no bearing knock, the turbo/oil system overhaul is viable. However, if the engine has been run for any period with mayonnaise (oil/coolant mix), we strongly recommend full replacement. Bearing damage is almost inevitable.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt 3.0L CRD engines with uprated aluminium oil coolers

problems.h4: 1.3L Turbo FireFly – MultiAir & Turbo Wastegate Failure
problems.affectedModels: Jeep Renegade (BU, 2019–present, 1.3L Turbo FireFly), Compass (2019–present, 1.3L Turbo FireFly)
problems.rootCause: The FireFly 1.3L uses a turbocharged MultiAir system similar to the Tigershark but with higher thermal stress. The MultiAir unit fails due to oil coking, and the turbo wastegate actuator seizes, causing overboost or complete boost loss.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace MultiAir brick, oil and filter change with correct 0W‑30 spec, reset adaptations | Restores valve control.
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable for early misfire or rough running with no turbo symptoms.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace turbocharger (or wastegate actuator if separately available), oil feed and drain lines, gaskets | Required if wastegate is seized (underboost/overboost codes). Often combined with MultiAir replacement.

| Supply & fit rebuilt engine with updated MultiAir unit, new turbo, ceramic-coated wastegate, 12‑month warranty | Recommended if both MultiAir and turbo have failed, or if the engine has suffered internal damage (bent valves, piston ring land failure).

problems.recommendation: The 1.3L FireFly is a young engine in the UK market, but MultiAir failures are already appearing. If you have a Renegade or Compass with under 60k miles and a single failure (either MultiAir or turbo), the minor repair is cost-effective. However, if both systems have failed or the vehicle has been driven with a misfire for some time, go straight to a full replacement — the labour to do MultiAir + turbo separately exceeds the incremental cost of a full rebuilt engine.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 1.3L FireFly engine prices for your Renegade or Compass

engineTypes.h2: Jeep Engine Types – Options, Costs & What to Expect
engineTypes.intro: Every Jeep engine type available through our UK network, with typical price ranges and recommendations based on your vehicle’s age, condition and your budget.

types.title: Jeep Reconditioned Engines (Recon)
types.description: A stripped, cleaned and fully inspected engine where all worn components are replaced with OEM‑spec parts. Best for owners who want factory reliability without the factory price.
types.priceRange: Typical price range: £1,800 – £5,500 (depending on engine code)

types.title: Jeep Rebuilt Engines
types.description: A complete rebuild with upgraded components addressing known failure points – such as revised Pentastar cylinder heads, uprated MultiJet II timing chains, or non‑MDS HEMI lifters – for a longer service life than a standard replacement.

types.title: Used Jeep Engines
types.description: A tested, low‑mileage take‑out unit from a donor vehicle. The most affordable option when budget is the priority, with verified history and warranty available.

types.title: Jeep Remanufactured Engines
types.description: A factory‑spec, as‑new engine: every surface is machined, every component is replaced, and the unit is built to meet exact OEM tolerances. The premium choice for performance models or owners wanting a zero‑mile engine.

types.title: Jeep Refurbished Engines
types.description: A lightly serviced, cleaned, and tested engine that works reliably but hasn’t had a complete rebuild. Often a pragmatic, fast solution for older or budget‑sensitive models where a full recon isn’t economical.

types.title: Jeep Supply & Fit Engines
types.description: Your replacement engine delivered and professionally installed by a UK Jeep specialist. Includes removal of the old unit, fitting, fluids, and a workmanship warranty.
types.priceRange: Typical added cost: £600 – £1,500 on top of engine price
types.cta: Find Jeep engine supply & fit deals

engineTypes.closing: All engine types include a minimum 12‑month warranty. Prices are typical UK market ranges and depend on exact engine code, supplier, and any additional fitting services. Use our free comparison tool to get a tailored quote for your Jeep model today.

engineSizes.h2: Jeep Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every Jeep engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: Jeep 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: Jeep’s 2.0L diesel appears across the Cherokee KL, Compass, and Renegade, using the Fiat Chrysler 2.0L MultiJet II (AJD/AJE family). Produced from 2014 to 2022, this four‑cylinder turbo diesel delivers strong economy, but timing chain stretch and EGR/DPF coking are well‑documented failure points that frequently lead to replacement demand, especially on vehicles used for short trips
items.cta: Get quotes for Jeep 2.0L diesel engines
commonFailurePoints: EGR valve and intake manifold coking

items.title: Jeep 2.2L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.2L MultiJet II is Jeep’s higher‑torque diesel, fitted primarily to the Cherokee KL, Renegade, and Compass from 2014 onward. This engine shares the same timing chain and EGR weaknesses as the 2.0L diesel but with additional turbo actuator failures and oil cooler gasket leaks. Replacement demand is high for vehicles exceeding 80,000 miles, particularly where timing chain rattle or EGR‑related limp mode appears
items.cta: Get quotes for Jeep 2.2L diesel engines

items.title: Jeep 2.8L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.8L CRD diesel was Jeep’s heavy‑duty option for the Wrangler JK and earlier Grand Cherokee (WK) models. Using the VM Motori RA 428 engine, this four‑cylinder turbo diesel is generally robust, but injector failure (particularly Bosch piezo units), turbo overboost (stuck VNT vanes), and cylinder head cracking around the glow plug holes are known replacement drivers. Later Wrangler JK examples (2011–2016) saw fewer failures but still suffer from EGR cooler leaks
items.cta: Get quotes for Jeep 2.8L diesel engines

items.title: Jeep 3.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 3.0L CRD diesel – VM Motori A 630 (OM642 derivative) – was fitted to the Grand Cherokee WK2 from 2011 to 2019 and later models of the Cherokee (pre‑2014). This Mercedes‑based V6 turbo diesel offers refined power, but the plastic oil cooler housing is a critical design flaw: it cracks, mixing oil and coolant, leading to turbo failure and main bearing damage. Timing chain stretch (upper chain on the engine) and swirl flap failure are also common
items.cta: Get quotes for Jeep 3.0L diesel engines

items.title: Jeep 1.3L Petrol Engines
items.description: The turbocharged 1.3L FireFly (Fiat GSE family) arrived in the Renegade and Compass around 2019 as a downsized turbo replacement for older naturally aspirated units. This engine uses MultiAir electro‑hydraulic valve actuation, which is sensitive to oil quality. Failure of the MultiAir brick and turbo wastegate actuator are the top reasons for replacement, typically between 40,000 and 80,000 miles
items.cta: Compare Jeep 1.3L petrol engine prices

items.title: Jeep 1.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.4L turbo petrol – Fiat’s T‑Jet engine – was fitted to the Patriot and early Compass models for the UK market. This four‑cylinder unit is relatively simple but suffers from turbo oil feed blockages, ignition coil failures, and in some cases, piston ring failure leading to high oil consumption. Replacement is less common than JLR or BMW petrols, but well‑documented for higher‑mileage examples (70k+)
items.cta: Compare Jeep 1.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Jeep 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The naturally aspirated 1.6L E‑TorQ (Fiat/GEMA family) powered the Cherokee (KJ/KK) and Patriot in non‑UK volumes, but appeared sporadically in UK imports. The 1.6L is dependable, but valve guide wear (oil burning on overrun) and timing chain stretch at higher mileages (100k+) are noted failure points. Replacement demand is low relative to diesel or larger petrol sizes
items.cta: Compare Jeep 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Jeep 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: Jeep’s naturally aspirated 2.0L World Engine (GEMA family) appeared in the Patriot, Compass MK, and early Cherokee KL. It’s a non‑turbo unit with dual VVT, known for timing chain wear (especially the balance shaft chain), oil pressure switch leaks, and in higher‑mileage examples, piston ring failure causing significant oil consumption (up to 1L per 1,000 miles). Replacement is driven by chain jump or bottom‑end knocking
items.cta: Compare Jeep 2.0L petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: Timing chain and balance shaft chain stretch

items.title: Jeep 2.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.4L Tigershark (MultiAir II) is one of Jeep’s most common petrol engines, fitted to Renegade, Cherokee KL, Compass MP, and even the Fiat 500X platform‑mate. This four‑cylinder uses the same MultiAir electro‑hydraulic intake valve system as smaller FireFly engines. Failure of the MultiAir brick, burnt exhaust valves, and bottom‑end bearing wear are the primary replacement drivers. Oil consumption of 0.5L per 1,000 miles is normal in factory spec, but excessive consumption leads to catalyst damage
items.cta: Compare Jeep 2.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Jeep 2.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.5L AMC/Chrysler “Iron Duke” derived four‑cylinder appeared in older XJ Cherokees and TJ Wranglers (1990s to early 2000s). It’s a simple, pushrod design with mechanical fuel injection in later variants. Failures are rare, but cracked cylinder heads (between valves), camshaft wear, and oil pump failure at very high mileages (150k+) are documented. Replacement demand is low but steady for classic Jeep restorers
items.cta: Compare Jeep 2.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Jeep 3.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.2L Pentastar V6 (a stroked version of the 3.6L) was fitted to the Cherokee KL in limited numbers for the UK market. It shares the same VVL (variable valve lift) and cylinder deactivation system as the 3.6L, with identical weaknesses: rocker arm roller bearing failure, camshaft lobe scoring, and oil consumption from piston ring design. Replacement is driven by misfire codes that don’t resolve with rocker replacement, indicating camshaft damage
items.cta: Compare Jeep 3.2L petrol engine prices

items.title: Jeep 3.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The Pentastar 3.6L V6 is Jeep’s high‑volume petrol engine, fitted to Wrangler (JK & JL), Grand Cherokee (WK2, WL), Cherokee KL, and pickup variants. It dominates replacement demand due to oil consumption (early piston rings), timing chain stretch, rocker arm tick, and cylinder head failure (Left bank, exhaust valve seat drop on early 2011–2013 units). Upgraded replacement engines with revised cylinder heads and better piston rings are the recommended solution for any Pentastar over 80,000 miles showing symptoms
items.cta: Compare Jeep 3.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Jeep 2.0L Hybrid Engines
items.description: Jeep’s 4xe plug‑in hybrid system pairs a 2.0L turbo petrol engine (part of the GSE family) with an electric motor and 17.4 kWh battery. Fitted to the Wrangler 4xe (JL, 2021–present) and Grand Cherokee 4xe (WL, 2023–present), this system delivers off‑road electric capability. Hybrid‑specific failures include the electric coolant heater, high‑voltage battery cell imbalance, and the integrated dual‑clutch transmission (e‑motor integration). Replacement of the complete hybrid drive unit is rare but expensive. System / motor code(s): 4xe PHEV system (2.0L turbo GSE + e‑motor)
items.cta: Enquire about Jeep 2.0L hybrid engine availability

items.title: Jeep Diesel Engines
items.description: Jeep diesel engines cover many of the brand's highest-mileage UK models. Replacement demand is usually driven by timing system wear, injector and turbo faults, plus EGR or DPF-related damage once repeat short-journey use starts affecting reliability.
knownFor: Timing chain stretch (50k–90k miles)
EGR valve and intake manifold coking
DPF blocking in urban use
Timing chain stretch
importantNotes: Short-journey use increases DPF, EGR, and oil-dilution risk on many diesel engines.
A documented service history matters more on diesel replacements because injector, turbo, and timing faults often build gradually.
items.cta: Compare Jeep diesel engine prices

items.title: Jeep Petrol Engines
items.description: Jeep petrol engines span everything from core daily-driver models to higher-output performance variants. Most replacement enquiries start with timing chain noise, oil consumption, cooling-system failure, or internal wear that makes a warranted replacement more sensible than repeated repairs.
knownFor: MultiAir unit failure (valve control)
turbo wastegate actuator seizure
oil coking in cylinder head
Turbo oil feed blockage
importantNotes: Cold-start rattles, overheating history, and oil use are the biggest warning signs before a petrol engine replacement becomes necessary.
Direct-injection petrol engines often need specialist attention for carbon build-up alongside the main mechanical repair.

engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major Jeep engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK Jeep specialists.

entries.title: 3.6L Pentastar V6 – Petrol
entries.description: The Pentastar 3.6L (ERB, EXL, GWM, EXJ codes) powers Wrangler (JK 2012-2018, JL 2018+), Grand Cherokee (WK2 2011-2021), and Cherokee (KL 2014-2022). Common failures include cylinder head casting cracks (2011-2013 units), rocker arm bearing failure wiping camshaft lobes, and oil cooler housing leaks. Our rebuilt units include revised cylinder heads and upgraded rocker assemblies.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 3.6L Pentastar Jeep engine replacement

entries.title: 3.2L Pentastar V6 – Petrol
entries.description: Exclusive to Cherokee (KL 2014-2022, code unknown / part of Pentastar family). Shares all Pentastar weaknesses: rocker arm failure, camshaft wear, and early cylinder head issues on 2014-2016 units.

entries.title: 2.2L MultiJet II (EBU / EBV) – Diesel
entries.description: Found in Cherokee (KL 2014-2022), Renegade (BU), Compass (MP), and Wrangler (JL Europe). The 2.2L (200 hp) suffers from oil wicking through the engine wiring harness (contaminating ECU pins), timing chain stretch at 70,000-100,000 miles, and EGR/DPF clogging. Our rebuilt units include new timing components and revised harness sections.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 2.2L MultiJet II Jeep engine replacement

entries.title: 2.2L MultiJet II (EBN) – Diesel
entries.description: Lower-output version (138-170 hp) of the 2.2L MultiJet II, found in Cherokee (KL) and Compass (MP). Same failure patterns as EBU/EBV with added vulnerability to injector seal leaks.

entries.title: 2.0L MultiJet II – Diesel
entries.description: Used in Renegade (BU) and Compass (MP). Produces 140-170 hp. Known for oil wicking and timing chain issues common to the MultiJet II family.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 2.0L MultiJet II Jeep diesel engines

entries.title: 3.0L CRD V6 (EXL / EXF) – Diesel
entries.description: The premium diesel for Grand Cherokee (WK2 2011-2019). The Bosch CP4.2 HPFP failure is catastrophic – metal debris destroys injectors, fuel rails, and often the engine itself. Also suffers from injector clogging and swirl flap failure. Our rebuilt CRD units include complete new fuel systems and upgraded HPFPs where compatible.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 3.0L CRD Jeep Grand Cherokee engine replacement

entries.title: 2.8L CRD (ENS / ENC) – Diesel
entries.description: Powers Wrangler (JK 2007-2018), Cherokee (KK 2008-2013), and Liberty (KJ 2005-2007). Common failures include injector seal leaks (diesel in oil), turbocharger failure, and crankshaft position sensor issues.

entries.title: 5.7L HEMI (EZH / EZC) – Petrol
entries.description: Found in Grand Cherokee (WK2, WL), Commander (XK), and some Wrangler conversions. The signature failure is MDS lifter tick – collapsed lifters wipe out camshaft lobes, often on cylinder #1 or #7. Our rebuilt units include non-MDS lifter conversion and upgraded oil pumps.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 5.7L HEMI Jeep engine with non-MDS conversion

entries.title: 6.4L HEMI SRT – Petrol
entries.description: Used in Grand Cherokee SRT8 (WK2 2012-2021). Shares HEMI lifter issues but adds valve train stress from high-RPM operation. Replacement is expensive but essential for performance models.

entries.title: 6.2L Hellcat Supercharged – Petrol
entries.description: Exclusive to Grand Cherokee Trackhawk (2018-2021). 707 hp. Failures include supercharger bearing wear, high-pressure fuel pump issues, and overheating under track use. Very few UK specialists handle these.
entries.cta: Contact specialist for 6.2L Hellcat Jeep engine quotes

entries.title: 2.4L Tigershark (BUV / BUU) – Petrol
entries.description: Powers Cherokee (KL), Renegade (BU), and Compass (MP). The 2.4L is notorious for excessive oil consumption (1 quart per 1,000+ miles) caused by stuck piston rings, leading to catalytic converter failure. Our rebuilt Tigershark units include revised piston rings and updated oil control rings.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 2.4L Tigershark Jeep engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L GME T4 (Hurricane) – Petrol
entries.description: Used in Wrangler (JL) and Grand Cherokee (WL). 268-300 hp. Known for internal sand contamination from casting residue during early production years, plus occasional fuel injector failures.

entries.title: 1.3L FireFly Turbo – Petrol
entries.description: Found in Renegade (BU) and Compass (MP). 150-180 hp. Carbon buildup on intake valves due to direct injection causes cold-start misfires.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 1.3L FireFly Jeep engine replacement

families.name: Legacy Engines (Pre-2008 – still high replacement demand)

entries.title: 4.0L AMC I6 – Petrol
entries.description: The legendary straight-six found in Wrangler (YJ, TJ), Cherokee (XJ), and Comanche (MJ). Known for extreme longevity but suffers from rear main seal leaks and cracked cylinder heads (2000-2001 0331 casting). Our rebuilt 4.0L units include revised head castings.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4.0L AMC I6 Jeep engine replacement

entries.title: 3.8L V6 – Petrol
entries.description: Wrangler (JK 2007-2011) base engine. Known for oil burning, intake manifold gasket leaks, and cracked exhaust manifolds. A common swap candidate for owners upgrading to Pentastar or HEMI.

entries.title: 3.7L PowerTech V6 – Petrol
entries.description: Found in Liberty (KJ, KK 2002-2012) and Commander (XK base). Known for dropped valve seat failure (catastrophic), sludge buildup, and valve train noise.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 3.7L PowerTech Jeep engine replacement

entries.title: 4.7L PowerTech V8 – Petrol
entries.description: Grand Cherokee (WJ, WK 1999-2010) and Commander (XK). Failures include dropped valve seats, sludge from inadequate maintenance, and timing chain tensioner wear.

engineYears.h2: Jeep Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This section covers every major Jeep engine change across all UK-market models from the late 1990s to the present, organised by year so owners can identify their exact engine, understand when key powertrains were introduced or retired, and compare rebuilt or replacement engine prices from UK Jeep specialists.

years.preview: AMC 4.0 Inline-Six at its Peak; VM Diesel Arrives Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The late 1990s saw Jeep's legendary AMC-derived 4.0-litre inline-six reaching its mature, most refined form across the Cherokee and Grand Cherokee, while the VM Motori 2.8 CRD diesel arrived to give the UK market a viable oil-burner option.
keyChanges: 4.0-litre AMC inline-six final-generation spec confirmed across Wrangler TJ and Grand Cherokee WJ - Chrysler multi-point fuel injection now fully developed
Cylinder head casting updated circa 1998 on Grand Cherokee WJ; lower-flowing but more emissions-friendly design
VM Motori 2.8 CRD (R428) diesel introduced for European Grand Cherokee - Jeep's first serious modern turbodiesel in the UK market
4.7-litre PowerTech V8 introduced in Grand Cherokee WJ (1999) - overhead-cam architecture replacing the older 5.2 and 5.9 V8 options
Grand Cherokee WJ also available with 3.1 TD in some European configurations; 4.0 remains dominant petrol option
mainEngines: AMC 242 / ERH - 3,960cc inline-six petrol (Wrangler TJ, Cherokee XJ, Grand Cherokee WJ)
PowerTech 4.7 V8 - 4,701cc SOHC V8 (Grand Cherokee WJ)
VM Motori R428 (2.8 CRD) - 2,776cc turbodiesel (Grand Cherokee WJ diesel)
knownFor: AMC 4.0 inline-six is widely regarded as one of the most durable production off-road engines ever built - well-maintained examples routinely exceed 200,000 miles

years.preview: 2001 cylinder head casting concerns begin to emerge; the problematic 0331 casting fitted on certain WJ Grand Cherokee engines is crack-prone between cylinders 3 and 4
years.description: VM Motori 2.8 CRD is powerful but timing belt-driven - belt failure on this engine is catastrophic; strict interval adherence at 60,000-mile intervals is non-negotiable PowerTech 4.7 V8 offers strong torque but is less loved than the 4.0 six by specialists - sludging issues on neglected oil-change examples are documented
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Jeep Grand Cherokee 2.8 CRD engine replacement - Jeep 4.0 inline six engine for sale UK - Jeep Cherokee XJ 4.0 engine rebuild - Jeep Grand Cherokee 4.7 V8 engine specialist
years.cta: Compare 1998–2000 Jeep 4.0, 4.7 V8 and 2.8 CRD replacement engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: Grand Cherokee WJ Twilight; 4.0 Final Years Major Reliability Revision
years.description: The 2001–2003 period is the most critical in the 4.0-litre's history - the introduction of the problematic 0331 cylinder head casting makes this the only generation of Jeep's most iconic engine that specialists advise owners to inspect before purchase.
keyChanges: 4.0-litre fitted with revised 0331 cylinder head casting from 2001 - prone to cracking between cylinders 3 and 4; most serious known fault in the engine's entire production history
Grand Cherokee WJ continues with 4.7 PowerTech V8 and 2.8 CRD diesel alongside 4.0 petrol
Liberty (Cherokee KJ) launched in 2002 for some markets with PowerTech 3.7 V6 and 2.4-litre four-cylinder options
Wrangler TJ retains 4.0 six and 2.5 four-cylinder; 2.4 Tigershark replaces 2.5 from 2003 on SE models
Increasing Euro 3 emissions demands begin limiting 4.0's lifespan in European specification
mainEngines: AMC 242 / ERH (0331 head) - 3,960cc inline-six petrol (Grand Cherokee WJ, Wrangler TJ, Cherokee XJ final)
PowerTech 3.7 EKG - 3,701cc SOHC V6 (Cherokee/Liberty KJ)
VM Motori R428 2.8 CRD - 2,776cc turbodiesel (Grand Cherokee WJ diesel)
knownFor: 0331 head casting is the most important identifier for any buyer of a 2001–2004 Jeep 4.0 - check the casting number on the driver's side of the block where valve cover meets cylinder head
Despite the head casting issue, the 4.0's bottom end remains unimpeachable - block integrity and crankshaft durability are benchmark-level
VM 2.8 CRD timing belt failures increasingly documented in UK ownership from this era - many private owners unaware of critical belt service intervals
PowerTech 3.7 V6 in the Liberty/KJ is considered the weak point of that generation's engine offer - less torque, less character, and more expensive to rebuild than the 4.0 six
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Jeep 4.0 engine 0331 head replacement - Jeep Grand Cherokee 2001 2002 2003 engine for sale - Jeep 4.0 cylinder head cracked repair UK - Jeep Grand Cherokee WJ 2.8 diesel engine rebuild
years.cta: Get quotes for 2001–2003 Jeep Grand Cherokee and Wrangler replacement engines from UK specialists

years.preview: WK Grand Cherokee; 4.0 Retired, 3.0 CRD Arrives Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The all-new Grand Cherokee WK (2005) retired the beloved 4.0-litre inline-six for good, replacing it with the 3.7 V6 and 5.7 HEMI V8, while the 3.0 CRD Mercedes-Benz diesel became the most significant engine in Jeep's European history.
keyChanges: AMC 4.0 inline-six discontinued - final production year 2006 in the Wrangler TJ; after 19 years, Jeep's most iconic engine retires
Grand Cherokee WK (2005) introduces 5.7-litre HEMI V8 in Jeep for the first time - 325bhp, cylinder deactivation (MDS)
3.0-litre CRD Mercedes-Benz diesel (OM642 V6) introduced in WK Grand Cherokee for European market - a landmark European diesel for Jeep
Commander (XK) launched 2006 with same 3.0 CRD, 3.7 V6, and 5.7 HEMI options as WK
3.7 V6 PowerTech replaces 4.0 in entry-level WK Grand Cherokee and Commander
mainEngines: 5.7 HEMI EZH/EZB - 5,654cc OHV V8 MDS (Grand Cherokee WK, Commander XK)
3.7 PowerTech EKG/EKH - 3,701cc SOHC V6 (Grand Cherokee WK, Commander XK, Liberty KJ)
OM642 3.0 CRD - 2,987cc turbodiesel V6 (Grand Cherokee WK diesel, Commander XK diesel)
VM R428 2.8 CRD - 2,776cc turbodiesel (Liberty KJ, Wrangler TJ final diesel)
knownFor: 3.0 CRD OM642 is the best diesel Jeep ever sold in the UK - smooth, torquey, long-lived when timing chain is maintained; generates the highest replacement demand of any non-4.0 Jeep engine
5.7 HEMI V8 is characterful but thirsty (typically 18–22mpg UK real world); MDS cylinder deactivation solenoid failure is a recurring known issue
3.7 V6 is widely considered an inadequate replacement for the 4.0 six - less torque, rougher at high revs, no significant improvement in fuel economy
OM642 3.0 CRD big-end bearing failure on high-mileage examples (typically cylinder 5) is the most serious known failure mode; often caused by extended oil service intervals
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Jeep Grand Cherokee 3.0 CRD engine replacement - Jeep 5.7 HEMI engine for sale UK - Jeep Grand Cherokee diesel engine rebuild - Jeep Commander 3.0 CRD engine specialist UK - Jeep OM642 engine bearing failure
years.cta: Compare 2004–2006 Jeep Grand Cherokee and Commander replacement engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: Wrangler JK Arrives; 3.0 CRD Dominant in Europe
years.description: The Wrangler JK (2007) brought the 3.8-litre V6 to the iconic off-roader while the Grand Cherokee and Commander continued with the 3.0 CRD as the UK's default Jeep diesel across the entire premium range.
keyChanges: Wrangler JK (2007) launches with 3.8-litre EGH V6 - replacing the legendary 4.0 inline-six; a change that divided Jeep's global fanbase
Grand Cherokee WK continues through facelift with 3.0 CRD OM642 and 5.7 HEMI; 6.1 SRT8 added for high-performance variant
6.1-litre HEMI SRT8 (370bhp) introduced in Grand Cherokee - Jeep's first track-oriented derivative
Commander XK continues unchanged with existing engine lineup
Patriot and Compass (MK, MP) arrive from 2007 with 2.0 and 2.4 petrol plus 2.0 CRD diesel - first small Jeeps for UK buyers
Euro 4 compliance confirmed across the UK range by 2008 MY
mainEngines: EGH 3.8 V6 - 3,778cc OHV V6 (Wrangler JK)
OM642 3.0 CRD - 2,987cc turbodiesel V6 (Grand Cherokee WK, Commander XK)
5.7 HEMI EZB - 5,654cc V8 MDS (Grand Cherokee WK continued)
6.1 SRT HEMI ESF - 6,059cc V8 (Grand Cherokee SRT8)
2.0 CRD / 2.4 - 1,968cc diesel / 2,360cc petrol (Patriot, Compass MK)
knownFor: 3.8 V6 in the Wrangler JK is universally regarded as the model's biggest weakness - underpowered for the JK's weight, prone to overheating in low-speed off-road use, and with a known excessive oil consumption problem

Grand Cherokee 3.0 CRD continues to be the UK's most wanted Jeep engine - smooth, refined, highly capable, but big-end bearing risk increases notably beyond 120,000 miles without correct oil and service records
Patriot and Compass 2.0 CRD is a Volkswagen-sourced unit (BWC/BMM); reasonably reliable but injector and EGR failure rates are typical for this era of Euro 4 VW Group diesels
JK Wrangler 3.8 specialist rebuild demand is high - many owners proactively replace with the later 3.6 Pentastar as a more capable power unit

years.cta: Get quotes for 2007–2010 Jeep Wrangler and Grand Cherokee replacement engines from UK specialists

years.preview: Pentastar V6 Launches: Wrangler JK Transformed Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The 3.6-litre Pentastar V6 (2012 Wrangler JK) was one of the most important engine introductions in Jeep's modern history - completely replacing the weak 3.8, producing 285bhp, and becoming the brand's dominant petrol powerplant across all major models.
keyChanges: Pentastar 3.6-litre V6 (ERB engine code) introduced in Wrangler JK from 2012 MY - 285bhp, a transformative improvement over the outgoing 3.8
Grand Cherokee WK2 (2011 UK) launches with Pentastar 3.6 V6, 5.7 HEMI V8, and updated 3.0 CRD V6 diesel
Grand Cherokee SRT8 updated with 6.4 HEMI (470bhp) from 2012 - replaces the 6.1 unit
Dodge-sourced EcoDiesel 3.0 V6 (VM Motori) starts transition process in Grand Cherokee
Euro 5 compliance achieved across UK range - significant for diesel emissions management
mainEngines: ERB Pentastar 3.6 V6 - 3,604cc DOHC V6 (Wrangler JK, Grand Cherokee WK2)
5.7 HEMI Eagle EZH - 5,654cc V8 MDS (Grand Cherokee WK2)
6.4 SRT HEMI - 6,424cc V8 (Grand Cherokee SRT8)
OM642 3.0 CRD V6 - 2,987cc turbodiesel (Grand Cherokee WK2 diesel continued)
knownFor: Pentastar 3.6 is a genuine step-change for the Wrangler - owners report dramatically improved on-road performance, better fuel economy, and significantly improved refinement vs. 3.8
Oil cooler failure is the Pentastar's primary weakness - typically presenting at 70,000–120,000 miles, causing oil and coolant mixing; requires prompt attention to avoid internal damage
Rocker arm / cam follower failure causes a distinctive ticking noise at start-up - a known Pentastar characteristic from approximately 60,000–100,000 miles requiring specialist inspection
Grand Cherokee 3.0 CRD continues as the UK diesel default; big-end bearing risk on cylinder 5 remains the most serious high-mileage failure for this otherwise excellent unit

years.cta: Compare 2011–2013 Jeep Pentastar V6 and Grand Cherokee replacement engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: Renegade & Cherokee Small-Displacement Era; EcoDiesel Arrives Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Jeep's partnership with Fiat Chrysler brought the Italian-engineered small-SUV platform to the UK with the Renegade and Cherokee KL, introducing the MultiAir 1.4 Turbo, Tigershark 2.4, and Fiat 2.0 MultiJet diesel - the most wholesale engine-family change in Jeep's European history.
keyChanges: Jeep Renegade (BU) launches 2015 with Fiat-sourced 1.4 MultiAir Turbo (140bhp) and 2.0 MultiJet diesel (140bhp) - Jeep's first urban-SUV engines
Cherokee KL (2014) replaces Liberty with Tigershark 2.4 petrol (184bhp) and 2.0 MultiJet diesel - and controversially drops the V6 diesel from entry spec
Grand Cherokee gains 3.0 EcoDiesel (VM Motori V6, R630 DOHC) from 2014 - new variant of the VM diesel family
Compass and Patriot continue with ageing 2.0/2.4 petrol and 2.0 CRD diesel in parallel
Euro 6 compliance begins transition - Renegade and Cherokee KL launch Euro 6-ready
mainEngines: Fiat 1.4 MultiAir Turbo - 1,368cc turbocharged petrol (Renegade)
Fiat 2.0 MultiJet / 2.0 MJD - 1,956cc turbodiesel (Renegade, Cherokee KL)
Tigershark 2.4 - 2,360cc DOHC (Cherokee KL petrol)
VM R630 EcoDiesel 3.0 V6 - 2,987cc turbodiesel (Grand Cherokee WK2 EcoDiesel)
ERB Pentastar 3.6 / 5.7 HEMI - continued (Grand Cherokee, Wrangler JK)
knownFor: Fiat 1.4 MultiAir Turbo is the weakest link in the Renegade range - excessive oil consumption, engine stalling under acceleration, and coking of variable valve timing actuators are extensively reported
Grand Cherokee EcoDiesel VM V6 carries a concerning bearing reliability history at high mileage - big-end bearing failure in this generation follows similar patterns to the earlier OM642
Tigershark 2.4 in the Cherokee KL has a documented excessive oil consumption problem - owners advised to check levels every 1,000 miles on high-mileage examples
Cherokee KL's 9-speed automatic transmission draws more owner criticism than its engines - but transmission-related stress accelerates wear on MultiJet diesel seals and mounts

years.preview: New Compass, 1.3 GSE Turbo & Euro 6 Consolidation Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The second-generation Compass (MP) launched in 2017 as the most important small Jeep in UK history, bringing the new Fiat/GM co-developed 1.3-litre GSE turbocharged petrol that would become the default engine across Renegade, Compass, and Cherokee over the following years.
keyChanges: Compass Mk2 (MP, 2017 UK) debuts with 1.3 GSE Turbo (130–150bhp) and 1.6 MultiJet diesel - replaces outgoing Patriot and Compass MK entirely
1.3 GSE T4 (150bhp) also introduced in Renegade facelift - Fiat/GM co-developed unit shared with Alfa Romeo and Fiat
Cherokee KL continues with MultiJet diesel and 2.4 Tigershark petrol through this period
Wrangler JK final edition 2018; Wrangler JL (all-new) launches with 2.0 Turbo inline-four (Hurricane) and Pentastar 3.6 V6 options
Grand Cherokee WK2 continues with Pentastar V6, 5.7 HEMI, and updated 3.0 EcoDiesel
Full Euro 6 compliance achieved across the entire UK Jeep range
mainEngines: 1.3 GSE T4 Turbo - 1,332cc turbocharged petrol (Compass MP, Renegade facelift)
1.6 MultiJet - 1,598cc turbodiesel (Compass MP diesel)
2.0 Turbo Hurricane - 1,995cc turbocharged petrol (Wrangler JL)
2.2 MultiJet - 2,184cc turbodiesel (Cherokee KL facelift)
ERB Pentastar 3.6 / EZH 5.7 HEMI - continued (Grand Cherokee, Wrangler JL)
knownFor: 1.3 GSE Turbo carries a mixed reliability reputation - engine stalling under hard acceleration on early units, and oil consumption concerns from 40,000+ miles reported across Compass and Renegade forums

Wrangler JL 2.0 Turbo is a genuine improvement over the earlier 3.8 V6 in terms of power delivery - but its complex oil separator system requires attention; separator failure leads to oil ingestion into the intake
Pentastar 3.6 oil cooler and rocker arm issues documented throughout this generation - now well-understood by UK specialists who have developed standard preventative procedures
2.2 MultiJet in the Cherokee KL is the most composed diesel Jeep offered in this period - smooth, Euro 6-clean, with relatively limited failure history in early ownership

years.preview: 4xe Plug-In Hybrid Launch: Wrangler and Grand Cherokee Electrified Technology Introduction
years.description: The Wrangler 4xe (2021) and Grand Cherokee 4xe (2022) marked Jeep's first electrification in the UK, pairing the 2.0-litre Turbo with dual electric motors and a 17.3kWh battery for off-road PHEV performance - a landmark in both Jeep's history and the UK 4x4 market.
keyChanges: Wrangler 4xe (2021) combines 2.0 Turbo Hurricane with two electric motors - combined 375bhp, up to 21 miles EV-only range; 17.3kWh battery
Grand Cherokee WL (all-new, 2022 UK) launches exclusively as 4xe PHEV in UK - 2.0 Turbo + dual eMot = 380bhp combined; 25 miles EV range
Grand Cherokee WL 4xe becomes first Jeep eligible for UK Plug-in Car Grant at launch
Renegade and Compass gain mild e-Hybrid (MHEV) 1.5-litre GSE T3 option - belt-integrated starter-generator, 48V
All new UK Jeep diesel variants progressively discontinued - EcoDiesel Grand Cherokee quietly withdrawn
mainEngines: 2.0 Turbo Hurricane + 4xe eMot - 1,995cc turbo + dual motors 375–380bhp combined (Wrangler 4xe, Grand Cherokee 4xe)
1.5 GSE T3 MHEV - 1,499cc turbo petrol + 48V MHEV (Compass, Renegade MHEV)
1.3 GSE T4 - 1,332cc turbo petrol (Compass, Renegade continuation)
ERB Pentastar 3.6 - continued (Wrangler JL non-PHEV, US-spec Gladiator)
knownFor: Wrangler 4xe is the first credible off-road PHEV - the added torque of the electric motors improves rock-crawl capability as well as road performance
4xe battery management on older WK2 Grand Cherokee platforms is complex; Stellantis-trained or specialist independent diagnostics essential for any hybrid drivetrain fault
1.5 GSE T3 MHEV in Compass and Renegade is an improvement over the 1.3 in NVH terms, but the 48V belt-integrated system adds a new service item not present in earlier petrol variants

faq.h2: Jeep Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: ** Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Jeep owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Jeep engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A complete Jeep engine replacement typically costs between £2,500 and £4,500 for common four‑cylinder diesel units like the 2.0L MultiJet II (Renegade, Compass) and £4,000 to £6,500 for the 3.6L Pentastar V6 (Wrangler, Grand Cherokee). Reconditioned engines usually sit in the middle of those ranges, while premium remanufactured units with upgraded components (e.g., revised piston rings on the Pentastar, uprated chains on the MultiJet) can reach £5,500–£8,000. Supply‑and‑fit adds £400–£900 to the engine price depending on the model. For an accurate price tailored to your registration number and engine code, use our free comparison tool. *
items.cta: Compare Jeep engine replacement prices now*

items.question: What affects the cost of a Jeep engine replacement?
items.answer: Four key factors determine the final price: engine size and complexity (a 3.0L CRD V6 diesel costs significantly more than a 1.3L FireFly petrol); condition – used, reconditioned, or remanufactured; vehicle age and complexity (Wrangler JL access is easier than Grand Cherokee WK2, affecting labour hours); and supply‑only vs supply‑and‑fit. Geographic location also matters – London and South‑East labour rates can be 20–30% higher than the national average. Our marketplace shows you transparent, all‑in quotes from multiple UK Jeep specialists so you can see exactly what affects each price. *
items.cta: Get quotes from UK Jeep engine specialists*

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Jeep?
items.answer: In most cases, yes – especially if the chassis, transmission, and bodywork are in good condition. A properly rebuilt Jeep engine with upgraded components (e.g., improved piston rings on a Pentastar, an aluminium oil cooler on a 3.0L CRD) often outlasts the original. Compare that to spending £15,000–£30,000 on a replacement vehicle of similar age and spec. For sought‑after models like the Wrangler JK, Grand Cherokee WK2, or a well‑maintained Cherokee XJ, engine replacement is almost always financially sensible. The only time we advise against it is if the rest of the vehicle has severe structural rust or irreparable electrical faults. *
items.cta: Should I rebuild or replace my Jeep engine? Compare both options*

items.question: What's included in a Jeep engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: A full supply‑and‑fit service includes sourcing the correct engine (by engine code, not just model), removal of the old unit, installation of the replacement, all new gaskets, seals, fluids and filters, a workmanship warranty (typically 12 months), and disposal of the old engine. Most specialists also include a road test and final inspection. Fitting costs generally range from £450–£900 depending on the model – the Grand Cherokee WK2 is towards the upper end due to its complex front subframe design. Always confirm what’s included before committing: some quotes exclude ancillaries like the alternator, starter, or turbo. *
items.cta: Compare Jeep engine supply and fit deals from UK specialists*

items.question: How long does a Jeep engine replacement take?
items.answer: For common models like the Cherokee KL, Compass MP, or Renegade with MultiJet II or Tigershark engines, a straightforward swap takes 3–5 working days. More complex models – especially the Wrangler JK (2.8L CRD) or Grand Cherokee WK2 (3.0L CRD or 3.6L Pentastar) – can take 5–7 days due to accessory removal and subframe alignment. The biggest variable is engine availability: our network of UK specialists holds stock of the most commonly replaced codes (e.g., Pentastar 3.6L, MultiJet 2.0L/2.2L, 3.0L CRD), so lead times are often shorter than going to a main dealer. Ask each supplier for their current turnaround when you request a quote. *
items.cta: Find a Jeep engine specialist with fast turnaround*

items.question: Do reconditioned Jeep engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes – every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty as standard. Rebuilt and remanufactured units (the highest grade, with all critical components replaced to factory spec) often come with extended 24‑month or 36‑month coverage. The warranty typically covers major internal components: crankshaft, connecting rods, pistons, piston rings, cylinder head, valves, camshafts, timing chain or belt, and oil pump. It usually excludes ancillary parts (injectors, turbo, alternator) unless explicitly rebuilt as part of the engine. Always confirm exact terms with your chosen supplier before purchase. *
items.cta: Browse reconditioned Jeep engines with comprehensive warranty cover*

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Jeep engine?
items.answer: Used engines are cheaper upfront – typically £1,200–£2,500 for common Jeep units – but they come with unknown history and minimal warranty (often 30–90 days). They’re a good choice for a temporary fix or if you plan to sell the vehicle quickly. Reconditioned engines cost more (£2,500–£5,500) but include new piston rings, bearings, gaskets, and often upgraded components that address the original failure point (e.g., revised timing chain tensioners on MultiJet II, uprated oil cooler on 3.0L CRD). For long‑term ownership – especially on failure‑prone engines like the 2.2L MultiJet or early 3.6L Pentastar – a reconditioned or remanufactured engine with a 12‑24 month warranty is almost always the better investment. *
items.cta: Compare used vs recon Jeep engine prices side by side*

items.answer: EngineMarket connects you with a UK‑wide network of independent Jeep engine specialists, from Scotland to the South Coast. While there’s no single dominant regional hub for Jeep in the way Land Rover has the Midlands, we see strong clusters of 4x4 specialists around Manchester, Birmingham, Bristol, and Essex – many of whom benchmark their work against main dealers but charge 30–50% less. Enter your registration number, and we’ll match you with up to three suppliers who stock your specific engine code and offer supply‑only or supply‑and‑fit. Nationwide delivery is available if you prefer to use your own garage for fitting. *
items.cta: Find a Jeep engine specialist near me – enter your reg*

items.question: What are the most common Jeep engine failures?
items.answer: The most common catastrophic failures driving replacement are: timing chain stretch on the 2.0L and 2.2L MultiJet II diesels (Cherokee, Renegade, Compass), often failing by 80,000 miles; oil cooler housing cracks on the 3.0L CRD (Grand Cherokee WK2), which mixes oil and coolant and kills the turbo and main bearings; excessive oil consumption and valve seat drop on early 3.6L Pentastar V6 engines (2011–2013 Wrangler JK, Grand Cherokee); MultiAir brick failure on the 2.4L Tigershark (Renegade, Cherokee, Compass) and 1.3L FireFly; and crankshaft bearing failure on the 2.8L CRD (Wrangler JK) following injector failure and fuel dilution. These failures rarely give enough warning for a cost‑effective repair – engine replacement is the most common outcome. *
items.cta: See common Jeep engine problems and compare replacement costs*

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Jeep engine?
items.answer: Always compare at least three quotes – prices for the same engine code can vary by £1,000 or more between suppliers. Consider used if your budget is tight and you only need 12–24 more months from the vehicle. Choose reconditioned or remanufactured for long‑term ownership, especially on failure‑prone engines where upgraded components prevent a repeat failure. Ask for supply‑only if you have a trusted local garage – you’ll save the specialist’s fitting markup. Finally, check what’s included: some headline prices exclude VAT, delivery, or critical ancillaries like the oil pump and timing chain. Our marketplace makes all this transparent so you can compare apples‑to‑apples. *
